title: Emergence of a non-bulk hexagonal Fe$_2$S$_2$ single layer via phase transformation
authors: Affan Safeer, Wejdan Beida, Felix Oberbauer, Nicolae Atodiresei, Gustav Bihlmayer, Max Wolfertz, Chiara Schlichte, Wouter Jolie, Stefan Blügel, Jeison Fischer, Thomas Michely
abstract: Two-dimensional materials can stabilize crystal structures that are absent from their bulk counterparts, offering opportunities for materials design. Here, we report the synthesis of a previously unknown hexagonal Fe$_2$S$_2$ single layer with $β$-CuI structure, a buckled layer of two vertically stacked FeS honeycomb lattices, realized by thermally induced transformation of single layer mackinawite grown on graphene/Ir(111). In situ scanning tunneling microscopy and low-energy electron diffraction reveal a transition from a tetragonal to a hexagonal lattice accompanied by distinct morphological and electronic signatures. The hexagonal Fe$_2$S$_2$ forms reproducibly upon annealing and represents a new structural motif within the Fe-S material family. First-principles calculations identify the $β$-CuI structure as most consistent with experiment. The calculations suggest that on-site Coulomb interactions and magnetic order are relevant to understanding the stability of the new 2D Fe-S compound. The preferred nucleation of single-layer mackinawite, despite being energetically disfavored, is speculated to result from its low edge energy, analogous to the 3D case. Our results establish Fe$_2$S$_2$ as a platform for exploring structural polymorphism in two dimensions and demonstrate that reduced dimensionality can stabilize crystal structures not accessible in bulk materials.
summary: The researchers successfully synthesized a previously unknown hexagonal Fe2S2 single layer by annealing a tetragonal mackinawite precursor on graphene/Ir(111). Using a combination of STM and DFT+U, they identified the new phase as having a beta-CuI structure. The study demonstrates that reduced dimensionality allows for the stabilization of crystal structures that do not exist in bulk iron sulfides. The stability of this new phase is closely linked to magnetic ordering and on-site Coulomb interactions.
main_problem: Investigating the emergence of a new 2D hexagonal Fe2S2 phase and understanding how reduced dimensionality and electronic correlations stabilize a crystal structure absent in the bulk.
main_result: The discovery of a hexagonal Fe2S2 single layer with a beta-CuI structure formed via thermal transformation of tetragonal mackinawite, where magnetic order and Hubbard U interactions are key to its stability.
method: Experimental characterization using MBE, STM, STS, and LEED combined with theoretical DFT+U calculations and cRPA for parameter-free Hubbard U estimation.
model_or_system: Single-layer and bilayer Fe2S2 polymorphs (tetragonal mackinawite and hexagonal beta-CuI structures) grown on a graphene/Ir(111) substrate.
key_observables: Lattice parameters (a and c), differential conductance (dI/dV) spectra, island morphology, and magnetic configurations (FM, AFM).
important_parameters: Hubbard U = 1.6 eV, exchange J = 0.63 eV, annealing temperatures up to 1020 K, and growth temperatures between 470 K and 620 K.
main_assumptions: Assumed screening of the Hubbard U from 4.9 eV to 1.6 eV due to the metallic Ir(111) and graphene substrate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows STM topography and height profiles of tetragonal mackinawite; supplementary figures include dI/dV spectra for single vs bilayer layers, structural comparisons with the C2DB database, and a side-view model of the bilayer structure.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 2D Fe-S system, characterizes the precursor tetragonal phase, describes the thermally induced phase transformation to the hexagonal phase, provides structural and electronic identification via DFT, and discusses the role of electronic correlations and substrate interaction in stabilizing the new phase.
why_interesting: The study explores how dimensionality and electron-electron correlations (Hubbard U) can fundamentally alter the phase diagram and stabilize new structural motifs, which is central to many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3. STM annealing sequence of Fe2S2. After growth of Fe2S2 at 350 K the sample was annealed in subsequent steps of 300 s at the temperatures indicated in (a) to (f). In (a), all visible islands are t-Fe2S2. In (b) and (c) ’h’ highlights all h-Fe2S2 islands while in (d) ’t’ identifies remaining t-Fe2S2 islands. STM parameters: (a) Vb = 2.0 V, It = 50 pA; (b) Vb = 2.0 V, It = 50 pA; (c) Vb = 1.3 V, It = 70 pA; (d) Vb = 2.5 mV and It = 35 pA; (e) Vb = 1.6 V, It = 60 pA; (f) Vb = 2.0 V, It = 70 pA. All topographs are 200 nm × 100 nm. STM data taken at 300 K.

Figure 4. LEED annealing sequence of Fe2S2. After the same annealing steps as in Figure 3 LEED patterns of the sample were recorded with an electron energy of 72 eV.
